coredata.dc:description: We report the synthesis of red-emissive carbon dots (SCDs) from Spinach leaves via a hydrothermal method. These SCDs exhibit strong red photoluminescence (PL), high quantum yield (QY), and excellent stability. Their unique optical features allow clear visualization of latent fingerprints (LFPs) and latent lip prints (LLPs), offering a sensitive and practical forensic tool. Incorporated into polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) films, the SCDs form flexible nanocomposites (NCs) with UV-blocking properties that extend food shelf life by minimizing moisture loss and microbial growth, as shown in strawberry preservation tests. SCDs serve as fluorescent inks for anti-counterfeiting (AC), generating patterns visible only under UV light for secure data encryption. Cytotoxicity assays confirm their biocompatibility, supporting safe application in consumer and biomedical products. The environmental friendliness of SCDs highlights their potential for diverse applications in security, food safety, and materials science. Future research will focus on expanding the scope of SCDs in emerging technological fields.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report the synthesis of red-emissive carbon dots (SCDs) from Spinach leaves via a hydrothermal method. These SCDs exhibit strong red photoluminescence (PL), high quantum yield (QY), and excellent stability. Their unique optical features allow clear visualization of latent fingerprints (LFPs) and latent lip prints (LLPs), offering a sensitive and practical forensic tool. Incorporated into polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) films, the SCDs form flexible nanocomposites (NCs) with UV-blocking properties that extend food shelf life by minimizing moisture loss and microbial growth, as shown in strawberry preservation tests. SCDs serve as fluorescent inks for anti-counterfeiting (AC), generating patterns visible only under UV light for secure data encryption. Cytotoxicity assays confirm their biocompatibility, supporting safe application in consumer and biomedical products. The environmental friendliness of SCDs highlights their potential for diverse applications in security, food safety, and materials science. Future research will focus on expanding the scope of SCDs in emerging technological fields.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have recently gained substantial interest as a versatile luminescent material, celebrated for their low toxicity, exceptional stability, water solubility, and high biocompatibility. Various synthesis techniques, such as laser ablation, chemical oxidation, electrochemical synthesis, microwave pyrolysis, hydrothermal carbonization, and ultrasonic synthesis, have been explored to produce fluorescent CDs [1–3]. Among these, the hydrothermal method stands out for its eco-friendly, straightforward approach, avoiding harsh chemicals and embracing renewable natural carbon sources, a practice increasingly valued for sustainable materials development. Spinach, with its rich folic acid content and functional groups like -COOH and -NH₂, presents an ideal candidate for CD synthesis, as these groups contribute to high QY and enable the production of strongly fluorescent CDs [4].

Recent advances in carbon dot (CD) technology have significantly expanded their application landscape through structural and optical tuning. Shell–core engineered carbonized polymer dots have shown superior photostability and tunable emission, offering enhanced performance in sensing, imaging, and optoelectronic devices [5]. Moreover, the development of high quantum yield CDs with tailored emission, such as yellow-emissive CDs used in blue-light blocking films, demonstrates the potential for environmental and health-related applications [6]. Biomass-derived CDs have attracted attention due to their sustainable sourcing, diverse surface chemistry, and broad functional versatility, making them promising candidates for applications spanning from biomedicine to anti-counterfeiting [7]. These advancements underscore the importance of structural design and raw material selection in optimizing the luminescent behavior and multifunctionality of CDs. Although chlorophyll, abundant in nature, exhibits near-infrared absorption and emission due to its porphyrin structure, its direct application as a fluorescent material is challenged by its instability and hydrophobicity, prone to degradation under light, acidic or alkaline conditions, and oxidation [8]. Inspired by the fluorescence (FL) properties of porphyrins, novel approaches have emerged, such as red-emissive CDs synthesized from holly leaves, which enable applications like Hg²⁺ detection in cells [9]. Similar studies on red-emitting CDs from sources like Chinese cabbage and mulberry leaves show potential for fabric manufacturing and as fluorescent probes, though the precise mechanisms behind red FL from leaf-based CDs in organic solvents require further investigation [10,11].

Fingerprints (FPs) are critical in identity verification, particularly in forensic investigations. The distinctiveness and permanence of FPs make them invaluable in criminal investigations, with each individual, even identical twins, having unique FPs [12]. Latent FPs are formed when a finger leaves behind natural oils, sweat, or secretions on a surface, often requiring enhancement techniques to become visible [13]. Traditional detection methods, such as fluorescent pigments or chemical reagents, demand costly equipment or involve hazardous chemicals, making them unsuitable for rapid on-site application. In contrast, dusting with fluorescent samples has proven effective for LFP development, with improved sensitivity and spatial resolution [14,15].

Over the past decade, fluorescent CDs have attracted attention for optical data storage and AC applications due to their affordability, eco-friendly synthesis, consistent FL, and biocompatibility. Despite extensive bio-imaging, drug delivery, and FL sensing applications, CDs have seen limited exploration in LFP detection, primarily due to challenges like interference from substrate auto-FL, FL quenching from aggregation, and suboptimal visibility [16–18]. To address these limitations, there is a pressing need for a simple, portable, safe, and reliable method to illuminate LFPs and LLPs using CDs, overcoming the constraints of existing methods.

The global problem of forgery and counterfeiting poses substantial risks to public health, safety, and economic stability. Counterfeit goods, documents, and pharmaceuticals threaten consumer safety and fuel demand for secure, innovative AC solutions [19,20]. Advanced materials for AC applications, including luminescent markers, Radio Frequency Identification (RFID) labels, and anti-theft intelligent tags, are constantly evolving. However, these systems remain susceptible to cloning due to predictable encoding mechanisms. Luminescent printing stands out for its simplicity and ease of handling, making it widely used in security applications where durability, eco-friendliness, affordability, and tamper resistance are essential [21,22].

Minimizing food waste is essential for sustainable practices, as a significant proportion of global food production is lost to spoilage. Fruits and vegetables, in particular, are prone to microbial contamination, leading to rapid sensory degradation [23]. Adequate food packaging, including antimicrobial and UV-blocking films, extends shelf life and maintains quality by protecting microbial growth and physical threats. Biodegradable polymers, such as PVA, have proven effective due to their excellent film-forming properties, though they require enhancement with nanomaterials to address UV vulnerability and moisture absorption [24–30].

This study synthesizes SCDs via a hydrothermal route and optimizes them for use as sprayable fluorescent markers on various surfaces. The synthesized SCDs successfully reveal Level I, II, and III fingerprint features, including the number, position, and shape of sweat pores. Few studies have examined CDs as fluorescent markers for LFPs and LPs through a spray method, making this approach particularly novel. Additionally, hydrogen bonding groups incorporated into the PVA matrix enable the formation of functional NCs, enhancing the properties and broadening the applications of SCDs@PVA films in food packaging. To our knowledge, this is the first report to explore Spinach-derived SCDs for applications in LFP and LP visualization, UV shielding, multi-stimuli responsive shape memory behavior, food preservation, AC, and cytotoxicity assessments. Table 1 provides a comparative overview of CDs derived from Spinach leaves and other natural sources, including their carbon sources, synthesis conditions, particle sizes, and applications [31–45].

In this study, we developed a one-step hydrothermal method for the sustainable synthesis of red-emissive SCDs and demonstrated their potential in diverse applications. The red-emissive SCDs minimized background FL interference and effectively countered aggregation-induced quenching. These SCDs formed multifunctional fluorescent nanocomposite films (SCDs@PVA) with potential applications in food packaging, UV-blocking, and shape memory technologies when incorporated into PVA films. This research introduces Spinach-derived SCDs as promising candidates for advanced forensic, food preservation, AC, and biotechnology applications.

Fresh Spinach leaves were collected from farmland in Tumkur District, Karnataka, India, were used as the primary raw material for this study. Additional reagents included ethanol, sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄), acetone, Triphenylphosphine (TPH), acetonitrile, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), hexane, toluene, 1,2-propanediol, water, cyclohexane, diethylene glycol, methanol, and triethylamine. PVA and deionized (DI) water were also used to prepare aqueous solutions. All chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

Fresh Spinach was cut into pieces and immersed in a 1:1 ethanol-acetone solution (v/v) for 24 hours to begin the synthesis. Excessive grinding was avoided to maintain the integrity of the extract. After this initial soaking period, the Spinach dispersion was filtered and concentrated to obtain a clear extract. The ethanol and acetone were then removed using a rotary evaporator set at 60°C, and the concentrated extract was freeze-dried to yield a green precursor powder. For SCD preparation, 0.1 g of the precursor powder was dissolved in 30 mL of ethanol to produce a clear solution. This solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and subjected to solvothermal treatment at 160°C for 6 hours. After natural cooling to room temperature, the SCD solution was vacuum-filtered through a 0.1 µm filter membrane. Dialysis was then performed for four days using a dialysis membrane to eliminate any remaining unreacted materials and impurities. Finally, the purified SCD solution was freeze-dried to yield the powdered form of SCDs, as shown in Fig. 1 a.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visualization of latent fingerprints and latent lip prints via a spray method
ce:section.ce:para: The optimized SCD solution at a concentration of 30 µM was tested for its effectiveness in visualizing LFPs on various surfaces. Fig. 1b and c illustrate the development and visualization of LFPs using this spray method. Fingerprint samples were collected from a 25-year-old male donor, who washed his hands with soapy water, dried them, and then pressed his thumb onto sebum-rich areas, such as the forehead, to ensure adequate transfer of natural oils. Lip print (LP) samples were also obtained from male and female donors of the same age group.

The SCD solution (30 µM) was evenly sprayed onto the collected FPs and LPs on various substrates, including stainless steel, aluminum foil, a metal scale, leather, plastic bottles, an overhead projector (OHP) sheet, and other metal surfaces. Under UV light at a wavelength of 365 nm, the SCD-treated prints became visible, and photographs were captured using a Canon EOS 700D digital camera. To enhance fingerprint detail and aid in rapid identification, the YOLOv8x deep learning algorithm was applied. This state-of-the-art object detection technique leverages recent advancements in computer vision, providing high accuracy and speed in recognizing fingerprint features. The YOLOv8x algorithm proved highly effective in extracting and enhancing fingerprint characteristics for precise, efficient analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of photoluminescent SCDs@PVA NCs films
ce:section.ce:para: To fabricate red-emissive SCDs@PVA NC films, varying concentrations of SCDs (0.02 to 0.08 wt.%) were incorporated into a PVA suspension. Initially, 2 g of PVA was dispersed in 20 mL of deionized (DI) water and heated to 90°C with constant magnetic stirring for approximately 30 minutes until a transparent solution formed. The solution was then allowed to cool to room temperature (RT, 26°C). Next, 1 mL of SCD solution was added to the PVA solution at 0, 0.02, 0.04, and 0.08 mg/mL concentrations. The combined mixture underwent alternating cycles of stirring and sonication for 20 minutes to ensure homogeneity.

For film preparation, 4 mL of the resulting PVA or SCDs@PVA mixture was pipetted onto clear petri dishes and allowed to air dry at room temperature for 3 days, facilitating film formation through controlled water evaporation. This procedure was repeated for each SCDs@PVA NC film sample. Film thickness was maintained at approximately 0.20 ± 0.02 mm to ensure uniformity across samples, as measured with an LCD digital Vernier caliper. The resulting films were designated based on their SCD concentration: PVA, 0.02 wt.% SCDs@PVA, 0.04 wt.% SCDs@PVA, 0.06 wt.% SCDs@PVA, and 0.08 wt.% SCDs@PVA. The NC films were stored in a controlled environment at 25°C and 75% humidity to preserve their optical properties and structural integrity, which were monitored over time, as shown in Fig. 1d.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical properties measurement of the developed PVA and SCDs@PVA nanocomposite films
ce:section.ce:para:
The mechanical properties of the developed PVA and SCDs@PVA NC films (0.02-0.08 wt.% SCDs) were evaluated using the ASTM D882-02 standard method to determine tensile strength (TS) and percentage elongation at break (%E). Testing was conducted on a Universal Testing Machine (Instron-3365, Integrated System Solutions, Bengaluru, India), where five samples (1×10 cm) for each composition were tested with an initial grip separation of 5 cm and a crosshead speed of 5 mm/min. The average values were then calculated for each material. The TS (in MPa) and %E were determined using the following equations:

The NC films' Water Vapor Permeability (WVP) was measured using a modified ASTM E-96 method. Samples were conditioned for 24 hours at 55% relative humidity (RH) before analysis. A 25 cm² film was secured over aluminum cups containing 10 g of silica gel. To maintain 75% RH, a saturated sodium chloride (NaCl) solution was placed within the desiccators, and the cups were positioned inside. The desiccators were then stored at 25°C. Weight changes were recorded every 24 hours over 8 days. The Water Vapor Transmission Rate (WVTR) was calculated using Eq. (3), where

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of PVA and SCDs@PVA nanocomposite coatings onto strawberries
ce:section.ce:para: Fresh strawberries were selected from a local grocery store for uniformity in size, texture, color, and shape. After washing and drying, strawberries were coated using a dip-coating method in a solution of 2 g PVA dissolved in 50 mL DI water containing 0.08 wt.% SCDs. Both coated and uncoated strawberries were then dried at room temperature (RT, 25°C) and stored under refrigerated conditions (-4°C). Photographs of the strawberries were taken at intervals from 0 to 8 days to assess preservation effectiveness, as shown in Fig. 1e. Additionally, 0.08 wt.% SCDs@PVA coating was applied to half of a strawberry to observe mold development. All experiments were repeated 4-5 times to ensure accuracy and reproducibility.

5 g of PVA was combined with 0.5 g of SCDs to formulate the AC ink. The SCDs were sonicated in the PVA medium for 60 minutes to achieve a uniform dispersion, followed by degassing in an inert circulating system for 10 minutes to remove any air bubbles. The resulting clear solution was used immediately as security ink. The ink was applied using an inkjet printer and a brush to demonstrate its application for creating secure AC tags.

ce:section.ce:section-title: QY measurement, long-term storage, and photo-bleaching
ce:section.ce:para:

where Φ denotes the QY, with subscripts "st" and "C" representing the standard and SCDs, respectively. k represents the slope derived from absorbance and integrated FL curves, and η is the solution's refractive index. Quinine sulfate (QS) in 0.1 M H₂SO₄ (QY = 54%, η=1.33) was used as the reference standard [46]. Six concentrations (<1 ppm) of SCDs were prepared by diluting the synthesized SCDs in deionized water. FL spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm for each concentration, and a graph was created showing the FL intensity against UV absorbance at 360 nm. The slope
k C

The cytotoxicity of SCDs was evaluated using the MTT assay, a standard in vitro method that assesses cell viability. SH-SY-5Y cell lines were used to measure the cytotoxicity of SCDs. Cells were seeded in a 96-well plate at a density of 5×103 cells per well and cultured for 48 hours at 37°C in a 5% CO₂ atmosphere. Subsequently, SCDs were added to the cell culture medium (DMEM) at final concentrations ranging from 0 to 150 µg/mL, with each concentration tested in triplicate. Wells containing only the medium served as blanks. Following a 48-hour incubation, the media was replaced under a laminar hood with 150 µL of 2% FBS medium and 150 µL of 5 mg/mL MTT solution. Plates were incubated for an additional 4 hours, after which the MTT solution was removed, and 200 µL of DMSO was added to each well to dissolve the MTT formazan crystals. Absorbance at 570 nm was promptly measured using a plate reader (Biotek Instrument, Winooski, VT, USA). Cell viability and cytotoxicity were calculated as the relative mean percentage ± SD using Eq. (6):

where Aexp represents the absorbance of the test sample, Actrl represents the blank (medium without cells), and Apos represents the positive control (medium with cells).

This study utilized various instruments to characterize the synthesized SCDs and their applications. Structural analysis was conducted using Powder X-ray Diffraction (XRD) with a Shimadzu diffractometer equipped with Cu K α radiation (λ=1.5406 Å, 45 kV, 200 mA), providing detailed structural information on the SCDs. Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) images, acquired with a JEM-2010 microscope at 200 kV, allowed observation of the SCDs morphology and particle size.

X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on an Escalab 250Xi spectrometer with Al Kα radiation (hν=1486.6 eV), offering insights into the elemental composition and chemical structures present in the SCDs. Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra were obtained with a Perkin Elmer spectrophotometer using KBr pellets, aiding in identifying chemical bonds and functional groups within the SCDs.

The optical properties of the SCDs solution were characterized using UV-visible absorption Spectroscopy with a Shimadzu UV-3600 UV-Vis-NIR spectrophotometer in a 1 cm quartz cuvette at room temperature. PL Spectroscopy was conducted with a Hitachi F-7000 FL spectrophotometer, enabling a detailed examination of the SCDs’ luminescent behavior. Additionally, a steady/transient FL spectrometer (FLS 980) was used to analyze the FL lifetime intensity decay, providing insight into the luminescence mechanism of the SCDs.

QY measurements were performed using a QE-2100 quantum efficiency system (Otsuka Electronics, Japan), offering a precise assessment of luminescent efficiency. Finally, developed LFPs and LLPs were documented with a Canon EOS 700D digital camera for analysis. Each of these characterization techniques contributed valuable information on the structural, chemical, and optical properties of the SCDs, building a comprehensive understanding of their potential applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural, morphological, and chemical characterization of SCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The crystallinity and graphitization of the synthesized SCDs were evaluated using X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis. A prominent peak at approximately 2θ=20.02° was observed, indicating the presence of the (002) lattice plane, a characteristic feature of carbon-based materials with some degree of disordered carbon atoms, as shown in Fig. 2 a. The nitrogen and oxygen-containing groups on the surface of the SCDs likely contribute to the observed graphitization level [47]. A weaker peak at 2θ=41.09° suggests partial graphitization in the SCDs [48]. Based on the (002) lattice plane, the inter-planar distance d of the SCDs was calculated to be 0.38 nm, slightly larger than that of bulk graphite (0.34 nm), likely due to the presence of nitrogen and oxygen functional groups at the SCD boundaries.

Raman spectroscopy was employed to explore the degree of graphitization further. The Raman spectra (Fig. 2b) showed nearly equal intensities for the D and G bands, indicating a comparable number of defects and graphitic carbon in the structure. The D band at 1333 cm⁻¹ is associated with sp³ defects and turbostratic carbon, while the G band at 1566 cm⁻¹ corresponds to sp² carbon ordering [49]. The ID/IG ratio from the Raman spectrum suggested a balanced presence of surface/edge defects and functional groups in the SCDs, indicating a robust carbon framework with functionalized edges. The functional groups in SCDs were analyzed using Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy. The FTIR spectrum (Fig. 2c) displays distinct peaks at 3423, 2918, 1636, and 1532 cm⁻¹, corresponding to the stretching vibrations of N–H, O–H, C=O, and C=C bonds, respectively [50]. Additionally, the band at 1469 cm⁻¹ is assigned to C–H bending vibrations, while the peaks at 1311 and 1271 cm⁻¹ are attributed to C–N stretching, 1234 cm⁻¹ to C–O stretching, and 796 cm⁻¹ to C–H out-of-plane bending [51].

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) revealed that the SCDs were spherical, uniformly dispersed, and exhibited minimal aggregation, as shown in Fig. 2d. Notably, the TEM image displays clear lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of 0.22 nm, which is consistent with graphitic carbon domains [52]. This phenomenon can be attributed to localized graphitization, a well-documented occurrence in carbon dots derived from biomass, even under relatively mild synthesis conditions. The particle size distribution (Fig. 2e) was determined by analyzing a statistically significant number of particles within the same TEM image. The histogram demonstrates a narrow size distribution, with an average particle size of 4.25 nm, confirming the nanoscale consistency of the SCDs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to analyze the chemical composition and oxidation states of elements in the SCDs, providing insights into the atomic environment. The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 2f) displayed peaks corresponding to C 1s, O 1s, and N 1s at 285.2 eV, 533.4 eV, and 399.2 eV, respectively [53]. The high carbon content and significant nitrogen and oxygen align with the anticipated composition of Spinach-derived CDs. Detailed deconvolution of the C 1s, O 1s, and N 1s spectra (Fig. 2g–i) provided further insights into the chemical environments. The C 1s spectrum (Fig. 2g) displayed peaks at 284.6, 285.2, and 286.4 eV, attributed to graphitic C–C (sp²), C–C defects/sp³, and C–O/C–N groups, respectively [52]. In the O 1s spectrum (Fig. 2h), peaks at 533.4 and 534.2 eV indicated the presence of O–C=O and C–O–C groups [54]. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 2i) exhibited peaks at 398.4, 399.2, and 401.3 eV, corresponding to C-O/C-N, C-NH-C, and NH₃⁺ functional groups, characteristic of Spinach compounds. Table 2 provides a quantitative overview of the surface chemistry of Spinach-derived Carbon Dots (SCDs), presenting the percentage of functional groups derived from the deconvoluted XPS spectra.

To further confirm the elemental composition of the SCDs, a selected SEM image and Energy-Dispersive X-ray Analysis (EDAX) were performed (Fig. S1a and b, Supporting Information). The EDAX spectrum distinctly reveals the presence of three major elements: carbon (C), oxygen (O), and nitrogen (N), without any detectable peaks for metallic or other inorganic impurities. This confirms the organic nature of the SCDs. The prominent carbon peak aligns with the carbon backbone of the SCDs, while the oxygen and nitrogen signals indicate the presence of various oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups, which are typically introduced during the green synthesis process. Further insights into the elemental distribution were obtained through elemental mapping (Fig. S1c–e, Supporting Information), which demonstrated a uniform and homogeneous dispersion of carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen across the SCD surfaces. The carbon map displayed intense and widespread signals, consistent with the primary carbon structure of the SCDs. Oxygen and nitrogen maps also showed even distribution, confirming the effective incorporation of heteroatoms throughout the structure. This uniform dispersion supports the formation of heteroatom-doped carbon dots with well-defined surface functional groups. These findings validate the high compositional purity of the SCDs, free from any metallic or inorganic impurities, highlighting their eco-friendly nature and suitability for a wide range of applications, including sensing, imaging, and biocompatible material development.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and differential thermogravimetric (DTG) analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The thermal stability of PVA and its nanocomposite (NC) films with SCDs was evaluated using TGA and DTG curves, as shown in Fig. S2, with relevant thermal data provided in Table S1 (Supporting Information). Each film exhibited two distinct degradation phases (Fig. S2a). The primary weight loss occurred between 220°C and 400°C for the pure PVA film. During this first degradation phase, side groups were cleaved, forming water and volatile organic compounds such as ketones, benzene, and aldehydes. As cross-linking increased within the cyclized structures, a more conjugated framework was created, enhancing thermal stability. The second degradation stage, occurring between 420°C and 490°C, was primarily due to random chain scission and cyclization, further breaking down the PVA matrix [55]. In the SCDs@PVA NC films, an initial weight loss phase between 180°C and 260°C was observed, attributed to the removal of phosphoric acid residues from the SCD solution and various surface functional groups, including -NH₂, -OH, and -COOH from the SCDs and side groups from PVA molecules. Furthermore, the thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) curve (violet-blue color) of the spinach-derived carbon dots (SCDs) is shown in Fig. S2a (Supporting Information) and reveals a multistage thermal decomposition profile. An initial minor weight loss (∼5%) observed below 150 °C can be attributed to the evaporation of adsorbed moisture and loosely bound surface water molecules. A more pronounced weight loss occurs between 150–350 °C, corresponding to the decomposition of oxygen-containing surface functional groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups derived from spinach biomolecules. Beyond 350 °C, a gradual and sustained weight loss continues up to 600 °C, indicating the degradation of the carbon core and more thermally stable nitrogenous species. The overall weight loss (∼60–65%) suggests the presence of abundant organic moieties, while the remaining carbonaceous residue confirms partial graphitization and good thermal stability of the SCDs. The DTG curves (Fig. S2b) reveal that with increasing SCDs content, the NC films' initial decomposition temperature (Tf) consistently decreased. This trend suggests that adding SCDs enhances the pyrolysis of PVA, likely due to an increased presence of side groups and phosphoric acid within the NC film. The second degradation phase, spanning 372 to 452°C, involved primary reactions such as chain scission, cyclization, and cross-linking within the PVA backbone [56].

As noted in Table S1 (Supporting Information), the thermal degradation temperature for all NC films during this stage closely matched that of pure PVA, with an observed increase in Ts values as SCD content increased. This indicates that the incorporation of SCDs significantly enhances PVA’s thermal stability. Moreover, at 600°C, the residual carbon content in the SCDs@PVA NC films showed a marked increase, rising from 20% in pristine PVA to 46% in 0.08 wt.% SCDs@PVA. This substantial char residue forms a protective barrier, limiting degradation by reducing heat flux to the underlying PVA matrix, further improving the film's thermal resistance [57].

The optical characteristics of the SCDs were evaluated using FL (FL) spectra and UV–vis absorption spectroscopy. Two distinct absorption peaks were observed at 335 nm and 496 nm, corresponding to the n-π* transitions of surface-bound functional groups and the π−π∗ transitions within the sp² aromatic domains, respectively (Fig. 3 a) [58]. The SCDs exhibited vibrant red FL, with maximum excitation and emission peaks at 496 and 610 nm, respectively [59]. The emission peak at 610 nm remained consistent across various excitation wavelengths, with peak emission intensity at 496 nm excitation. This alignment between the FL excitation spectrum and absorption curve suggests that surface states contribute to the red emission, with surface chemical groups generating FL through PL independent of excitation wavelength. FL analysis using the Commission International de l'Éclairage (CIE) diagram verified the SCDs’ red emission, with coordinates (0.645, 0.352) positioned distinctly in the red region (Fig. 3b) [60,61]. As the excitation wavelength increased from 440 nm to 580 nm, FL emission intensity initially rose, then decreased, while the peak emission wavelength remained fixed (Fig. 3c). The CIE coordinates were also used to confirm the PL color consistency of the SCDs, as shown on the CIE 1931 color space diagram (Fig. 3d).

The fluorescent stability and photostability of the SCDs were evaluated by monitoring their FL intensity over various aging periods, as shown in Fig. 3e. Remarkably, there were no significant changes in FL intensity from day 1 to day 30, indicating excellent stability. Furthermore, the SCDs showed no signs of photo-bleaching after 180 days, highlighting their resilience to environmental changes. Digital photos of aqueous SCD solutions, presented in the inset of Fig. 3e, confirmed this stability, with a consistent, brilliant red color emission under a 365 nm UV lamp over an extended period. The SCDs were exposed to high-intensity UV light at 365 nm to further test photostability for up to 90 minutes. Fig. 3f displays the outcomes, showing negligible differences in FL intensity between irradiated and non-irradiated samples, demonstrating the high photostability of the SCDs [62]. The FL lifetime characteristics of the SCDs were examined to provide insight into their optical behavior. The FL decay profile of the SCDs followed a tri-exponential model [63]:
(7)
