The decay curve (Fig. 3g) revealed three distinct decay lifetimes (T1, T2, and T3) with respective fractional contributions (A1, A2, and A3). This tri-exponential fit suggests two fundamental mechanisms: extended relaxation of excitons to ground states (T2 and T3) and direct electron-hole recombination (T1). Average lifetimes of 2.17 ± 2.455, 5.09 ± 2.917, and 9.01 ± 3.454 ns were obtained, with an overall average lifetime of 6.10 ns, indicating multiple emissive sites within the SCDs [63].

The absorption and emission spectra of quinine sulfate (QS) and SCDs at 350 nm excitation are shown in Fig. 3h and i. QS was selected as a reference for QY calculations, as confirmed by its absorption peak at 348 nm, closely matching the SCD absorption peak at 320 nm (Fig. 3h) [64]. The maximum emission for QS and SCDs at 496 nm excitation was also consistent, further validating QS as an appropriate standard (Fig. 3i). To minimize reabsorption effects, the absorbance was kept below 0.10. The integrated PL intensity and absorbance were plotted, and QY values were calculated using Eq. (5). The photoluminescence quantum yield (QY) of the synthesized SCDs was calculated using quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H₂SO₄ (QY = 54%) as the reference standard, following the procedure detailed in Section 2.8. As shown in Fig. 3h and i, a linear relationship was observed between the integrated fluorescence intensity and absorbance for both the standard and the SCD samples. The slope obtained from the SCDs (kC) and that from the standard (kst) were used in Eq. (5) to compute the QY. Based on this, the QY of the SCDs was determined to be 18%, indicating a reasonably high fluorescence efficiency suitable for optical and sensing applications.

The solution behavior of SCDs was also investigated across ten solvents with varying physicochemical properties, achieving stable dispersions in water, acetonitrile, cyclohexane, diethylene glycol, ethanol, hexane, methanol, toluene, 1,2-propanediol, and triethylamine after 1 hour of sonication. The SCDs demonstrated good solubility and consistent FL emission under 365 nm UV light across all solvents, as illustrated in Fig. 3j [65]. A slight reduction in FL emission was observed due to minor aggregation. Fig. 3k displays images of SCD solutions under UV light, showing bright FL in each solvent. Notably, while emission intensity in water was lower than in some organic solvents (such as ethanol or DMSO), it was sufficient to confirm SCD adaptability to aqueous environments.

The emission of SCDs in water underscores their suitability for water-based applications, making them promising candidates for biological imaging, environmental sensing, and other fields where aqueous conditions are prevalent. The FL signal in water highlights the potential of SCDs to retain their optical properties across different solvents, supporting their adaptability for diverse applications.

Fluorescent imaging has become essential for latent fingerprint (LFP) detection due to its excellent contrast and low background interference. Recent CDs advancements make them ideal for LFP development, particularly via solution-based approaches known for their simplicity and minimal dye usage. The synthesized SCDs, with their strong red emission and low toxicity, were utilized for LFP detection using a spray method [66].

For the LFP imaging shown in Fig. 4 a, the SCD solution was sprayed onto FPs for various durations to optimize visibility and resolution. The spray nozzle was held approximately 15 cm from the surface to apply a continuous, even mist, ensuring uniform coverage without oversaturation. Different spraying durations (3, 5, 7, and 10 sec) were tested, with a 5-second duration providing the best balance between coverage and clarity. This duration produced distinct, high-quality FP images with minimal runoff, ensuring the best resolution for fingerprint patterns. The sensitivity and selectivity of the SCD solution were evaluated on various surfaces, with Fig. 4a showing a comparative analysis of different SCD concentrations. To further assess LFP development, FPs on a porous substrate (paper) were sprayed with SCD solutions of varying concentrations. Under 365 nm UV light, the 30 μM SCD solution provided the most precise and most contrasted image. With increasing concentrations (1, 5, 10, and 20 μM), the FP gradually became more visible, achieving optimal sharpness at 30 μM.

To test the SCDs' ability to highlight specific LFP components, the letters "XYZ" were inscribed on a metal plate using solutions containing typical LFP components. These included glycine, lactic acid, glucose, sodium chloride, lysozyme, cholesterol, urea, and lipids (a mixture of lauric acid triglyceride and oleic acid) [67]. After drying, the inscriptions were immersed in a 30 μM aqueous solution of SCDs. Under 365 nm UV light, only the "XYZ" inscription with lipid components exhibited significant FL, indicating a preference for SCDs for staining lipid molecules in LFPs (Fig. 4b). This finding suggests that SCDs may adhere to LFPs via interactions between the lipophilic ends of SCD molecules and the lipid secretions in FPs. Electrostatic interactions between the positively charged SCDs and the negatively charged lipid secretions, including fatty acids, likely enhance this adhesion. Although SCDs do not fluoresce in aqueous solution due to non-radiative transitions, the viscosity of lipid secretions limits intramolecular movement, allowing the SCDs associated with lipid components in LFPs to emit intense FL (Fig. 4c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visualization of LFPs via spray method
ce:section.ce:para: Using an optimized SCDs solution (30 μM), FPs were effectively visualized on various substrates, including porous, semi-porous, and non-porous surfaces. Fig. 5 a and b display high-resolution red FL images of FPs on stainless steel, aluminum foil, a metal scale, and more complex surfaces such as leather, plastic, and insulation tape under 365 nm UV light without any post-treatment. The enhanced selectivity and specific adherence of the 30 μM SCDs solution are evident in the clear FP structures visible in the fluorescent images [68–70].

To investigate the SCD-stained FPs further, an experiment was conducted on aluminum foil sprayed with the SCD solution, left to air dry for 30 minutes. Images (Fig. S3a and b, Supporting Information) show numerous small red droplets, primarily concentrated along the FP edges due to the "coffee-ring" effect. As the water evaporates, the SCDs are drawn towards the edges, distinguishing sebum-covered regions from non-sebum areas. After 30 minutes of drying, most of the small droplets disappeared, leaving luminescent SCDs along the FP edges (Fig. S3a1 and b1 , Supporting Information). This retention of luminescence at the edges is likely due to FL quenching in areas where SCDs undergo a liquid-to-solid phase transition, while the presence of lipid molecules in FP residues may impede complete water evaporation.

The coffee-ring effect caused some SCDs to move towards the FP margins as the sprayed solution dried, creating an enhanced visual contrast between FP ridges and blank areas (Fig. S3c and d, Supporting Information). Electrostatic interactions between negatively charged fatty acids in FP residues and positively charged SCDs likely facilitated coating of sebum-covered droplets with SCDs, allowing FL to remain stable at room temperature for several days. Fig. S3e (Supporting Information) illustrates the FL staining process, where the SCDs’ resilience depends on both the coffee-ring effect and electrostatic interactions. Grayscale variations in the LFP images show a degree of contrast enhancement that, while aiding visibility, may not yet offer sufficient resolution for detailed Level I–III FP analysis.

FP ridge details are categorized into three levels: Level I represents general ridge flow and pattern type; Level II includes minutiae such as bifurcations and ridge terminations; and Level III involves finer ridge features, like pore structure and edge contour. Although SCDs enhance LFP visibility, further study is needed to determine their efficacy in resolving finer details required for comprehensive FP analysis. Pixel distribution analysis of selected FP regions (highlighted in white strips) was conducted using ImageJ software to assess the effectiveness of the 30 μM SCD solution in LFP visualization [71], as shown in Fig. 5c and d. The pixel analysis successfully differentiates ridge and groove areas based on high and low red values across all surfaces, demonstrating the potential of the SCDs solution for detailed FP visualization.

Fig. S4a (Supporting Information) shows LFPs on a paper substrate, visualized under 365 nm UV light after spraying. These images reveal distinct ridge flows, patterns, and orientations, highlighting the variation within FPs. Detailed views at different magnifications show Level I details (overall ridge flow and pattern type), Level II details (bifurcations, ridge ends, and scars), and Level III details (fine ridge elements like pore structure and edge contour) [72–74]. Visible even in fragmented or partial FPs, sweat pores are especially valuable for individual identification. Fig. S4b (Supporting Information) provides a 3D surface imaging view, showing SCD accumulation along the ridges and suggesting a strong binding affinity with FP residues [75]. Advanced analysis of a 3D plot of aluminum foil reveals Level I–III features, including core, ridge ends, islands, hooks, crossovers, double bifurcations, and sweat pores (Fig. S4c, Supporting Information). Close-up views highlight some tiny or partially closed sweat pores, while others are more open and prominent. These well-defined sweat pores are particularly important for accurate FP identification, especially in complex or incomplete prints [76,77].

Fig. 6 a–j presents an LFP on a glass substrate captured using scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The SEM images reveal that the SCD solution adheres selectively to FP residues, avoiding furrows. Parameters such as ridge end angles, sweat pore positions, ridge and furrow widths, and pore area were measured using ImageJ. Sweat pore locations were also analyzed for their openness and proximity to ridge edges. Fig. 6a–i and a1–i1 illustrate features extracted from SEM images and 3D interactive plots of LPs developed with the 30 μM SCD solution on glass substrates. Pixel analysis diagrams, displayed in Fig. 6j and j1, effectively separate LP ridge and groove regions using high and low red values, respectively. The images confirm that every ridge and groove area is distinct across both SEM and 3D plots, highlighting the suitability of the 30 μM SCD solution for LFP detection and visualization.

In individual identification through fingerprint analysis, key characteristics such as pore number, position, interspacing, shape, size, angle, and morphology are meticulously measured. A critical metric, average pore density, is the number of pores per unit length. Fig. 6k illustrates pore counts from SEM images of FPs revealed via the 30 μM SCD spray technique, showing a friction ridge pore density of approximately 14 pores per unit length [78]. Sweat pores varied in their positioning, with some centrally located or open at one or both ends. It’s notable that SCDs near pore edges may impact the perceived openness of a pore [79]. Factors such as pressure during fingerprint deposition, SCD uniformity, and development methods can alter pore characteristics, making it unlikely for two pores from the same finger to be identical. Fig. 6k and l display pore structures on ridge surfaces captured via SEM, revealing that closed pores are more prevalent than open ones, regardless of whether they are positioned on one or both sides.

Pore deformation due to factors such as skin elasticity and compression can alter pore shape, size, and composition, though inter-pore distances generally remain consistent. Even under pressure distortions or ridge course deviations, relative pore locations are preserved during skin flexion, allowing accurate measurement of inter-pore distances—the distance between the centers of two pores. Aligned pore centers on adjacent ridges form distinctive configurations, while pores along the same ridge create unique patterns specific to each individual.

The measured inter-pore distances and varied pore shapes, including ovals, squares, circles, and triangles, are depicted in Fig. 6k [80]. These shapes are not fixed and may change due to pressure, altering original structures. SEM images in Fig. 6 visually capture these diverse shapes, challenging the conventional view that circular pores predominate. This analysis reveals a broad distribution of shapes, with triangular, circular, and square pores commonly observed, while rectangular and rhomboid shapes are less frequent [81]. Quantified pore angles and areas, shown in Fig. 6l, further highlight the complexity and variability in human skin pore morphology.

The 30 μM SCD solution demonstrated remarkable efficacy in preserving LFP visibility on glass surfaces for up to 30 days at room temperature, as shown in Fig. 7 a. Under 365 nm UV light, FL imaging captured FPs with high resolution, and ImageJ software was used to analyze gray values along designated red line sections (Fig. 7a1). Notable differences in FL intensity between papillary ridges and furrows or the substrate highlight the high contrast in LFP visualization [82]. FL intensity differences in FPs persisted for up to 15 days with minimal change, though brightness declined afterward. The high sensitivity and enhanced contrast underscore the SCD solution’s utility in LFP detection [83].

LFPs on glass were also subjected to varied UV exposure durations (5-60 min), as depicted in Fig. 7b. Pixel intensity profiles in Fig. 7b1 show that ridge areas exhibit higher red values than furrows, with only a slight decrease in intensity as exposure time increases. Comparisons with different CDs used for LFP imaging, detailed in Table S2 (Supporting Information), demonstrate that SCDs offer superior resolution, color contrast, and stability compared to other reported materials, indicating their resilience to extreme environmental conditions.

The synthesized SCDs, characterized by robust red emission and low toxicity, were applied for LFP detection through a spray method. Their sensitivity and specificity were tested across various solvents, as shown in Fig. 7c. FPs developed with SCDs in solvents like acetonitrile and TPH showed fading and damage, whereas the 30 μM SCD solution alone produced high-contrast, accurate images under UV light. Pixel profile analysis of a small FP area (Fig. 7 c 1) distinguishes ridges with high pixel values from grooves with low values, indicating that the SCD solution preferentially binds to ridges over sweat pores, enhancing the clarity and contrast of FP detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FPs investigation using advanced machine learning techniques
ce:section.ce:para: The YOLOv8x deep learning model, known for its high accuracy, speed (over 60 frames per second), and efficiency, has become prominent in real-time object detection, notably outperforming other deep convolutional neural networks (DCNN) [84,85]. To enhance fingerprint (FP) feature detection, we compiled a dataset of various FP types created on porous, semi-porous, and non-porous surfaces. To identify finer features like bifurcations and ridge endings, bounding boxes were used to annotate images. Through extensive training using labeled FP images and manual annotations, the YOLOv8x network was optimized to recognize FP minutiae. The model was trained over 39 epochs, using rectified linear unit (ReLU) and sigmoid activations, on a V100 server with an Intel(R) Core(TM) i5-3470 CPU and 32 GB RAM. Post-training, the model effectively locates and identifies minutiae in challenging FP images, using convolutional and pooling layers to extract features and predict minutiae positions within bounding boxes. This geographic data can be further analyzed and compared against official databases.

This study introduces a fast and precise fingerprint (FP) identification method, supporting automated recognition in security, law enforcement, and biometrics applications. The automated FP recognition pipeline involves several key stages, illustrated in Fig. 8 . The initial pre-processing phase (Fig. 8a) prepares the input FP images for feature extraction, enhancing experimental accuracy by adapting the images to the model’s requirements. Essential tasks in this phase include normalization, resizing, binarization, and annotation, with an objective function that evaluates the model’s performance by minimizing errors through loss function analysis.

The model is then trained using four pre-processed FP images with pre-trained weights, as shown in Fig. 8b. Although there are similarities in object and image size, lighting conditions vary due to the application of SCDs. Convolutional neural networks (CNNs) detect FP features, ensuring accurate and comprehensive recognition despite these variations. In the prediction phase, the YOLOv8x model (Fig. 8c) refines initial predictions, which include bounding boxes, class probabilities, and confidence scores, to improve accuracy. Non-maximum suppression (NMS) reduces redundant or overlapping bounding boxes, retaining only the most confident detections. The final model output consists of bounding boxes and class labels for the detected FP features, which can be processed further for tracking, recognition, or categorization.

Fig. 8d–f display FPs created with different SCD solutions (30 μM) on porous, semi-porous, and non-porous surfaces under 365 nm UV light. FPs on non-porous surfaces demonstrated more explicit images with better contrast, underscoring the significance of substrate choice in FP analysis. Direct integration of FP images into deep learning algorithms enhances experimental accuracy for feature detection. Our novel YOLOv8x-integrated model, trained over 39 epochs for bifurcation and 49 for ridge end detection, efficiently identifies these features across various images (Fig. 8g–i). The model processes each FP as a unified object, achieving thorough coverage by employing a 7×7 grid that predicts five bounding boxes per cell.

Our YOLOv8x model leverages binarization during pre-processing (Fig. 8j–l) and uses logistic regression for bounding box determination with a confidence threshold of 0.3. Supporting Information (Figs. S5a–f and S6a–f, Supporting Information) compares the model’s performance with baseline YOLOv8x over multiple epochs. Precision and recall curves indicate improved accuracy, with precision, recall, and mean Average Precision (mAP) curves suggesting an optimal detection threshold starting at 0.7. Figs. S5e and f and S6e and f (Supporting Information) further highlight the model’s accuracy with minimal false or missed detections, as demonstrated by box loss (box_loss), classification loss (cls_loss), and distribution focal loss (dfl_loss) after 39 and 49 training epochs, respectively. Precision, recall, and F1 score curves at various confidence levels (Figs. S7 and S8 in Supporting Information) demonstrate optimal model performance at a confidence level 1. Precision-Recall (PR) curves for ridge end and bifurcation datasets, analyzed with the YOLOv8x model (Figs. S7b and S8b, Supporting Information), comprehensively assess the model’s feature recognition capabilities.

Visualizing FPs under 365 nm UV light emphasizes the effectiveness of deep learning in enhancing experimental accuracy through feature extraction. YOLOv8x achieves excellent contrast and accuracy on non-porous materials under UV exposure. While current FP methods are effective, our YOLOv8x model represents a breakthrough in real-time feature detection by predicting bounding box coordinates and class probabilities with speed and precision. Our DCNN model reliably predicts bifurcation and ridge end features through rigorous training and pre-processing, reducing errors and improving performance. This work introduces a robust and precise methodology for FP analysis, paving the way for advancements in forensic sciences and identification technologies.

For LP analysis, the LP was divided into four quadrants using an imaginary grid marked by a plus sign to assist in pattern observation. Each quadrant was individually examined, and the LP pattern type was recorded, as illustrated in Fig. 9 a. The combined pattern of all four quadrants represented the individual’s LP pattern, similar to fingerprint (FP) identification [86]. LPs provide unique identifying features that help differentiate individuals. Fig. 9b and c display LPs from a 25-year-old male and female, developed using a spray method with SCDs (30 μM) solution on a glass surface, visualized under 365 nm UV light.

Following the method by Domiaty et al. [87], the LP was categorized into four regions: upper left (UL), upper right (UR), lower left (LL), and lower right (LR). Unlike FPs, LPs consist of one-level features, which include Type I (complete vertical grooves), Type I0 (partial vertical grooves), Type II (branched grooves), Type III (intersected patterns), Type IV (reticular patterns), Type V (miscellaneous patterns), and Type VI (pores). These LP types are illustrated in Fig. 9b and c. These six features and their regions allow effective individual differentiation, demonstrating significant forensic value. Visualization of latent LPs using the SCDs (30 μM) solution shows excellent promise for forensic applications.

In this study, Type I (long vertical) was the donors' most common LP pattern, as shown in Fig. 9d, while Type I0 was the least common pattern in both male and female subjects [88]. The long vertical (Type I) and intersecting (Type III) LP patterns were most frequently observed in both genders. In male LPs, the pattern order was Type I > Type II > Type V > Type III > Type IV > Type I0 (least common). In female LPs, the pattern sequence was Type I > Type III > Type V > Type II > Type IV > Type I0, as shown in Fig. 9d. Interestingly, while Type V patterns were similarly frequent on the upper lips of both males and females, there was a significant difference on the lower lips, suggesting that the discrepancy in Type V frequency is primarily due to the lower lip, indicating a notable sex difference.

LPs were successfully developed on various surfaces, including OHP sheets, stainless steel, and aluminum foil, as illustrated in Fig. 10 a. Using the SCDs (30 μM) solution, LPs were clearly detected on all surfaces, overcoming challenges from background interference and light reflection. Under 365 nm UV light, initially invisible LPs became well-defined, revealing detailed groove patterns without background noise. Both upper and lower lip sections displayed distinctive LP groove patterns, influenced by factors like lip muscle structure, pressure, and impression angle, demonstrating SCDs’ versatility for individual identification. Grayscale analysis using ImageJ software (version 1.8, NIH) helped recognize groove patterns, as intensity profiles varied by position (Fig. 10b). These results confirm the effectiveness of the optimized SCDs for LP detection, positioning them as promising candidates for advanced forensic applications.

Further analysis included 3D interactive plots generated from surface profiles, showing varied intensity regions representing differences in LP thickness across surfaces. Dense and continuous patches in 3D plots indicated areas where adjacent LP lines merged (Fig. 10c). LP images and red pixel profiles were analyzed over 1, 15, and 30 days to assess FL stability (Fig. 10d). Minimal change was observed even after 30 days, with only a 20% decrease in red values, attesting to the stability of LPs with SCDs (Fig. 10e). Additionally, 3D surface plots showed intact LP grooves (Fig. 10f). A quadrant-wise analysis of LP features across different substrates and aging durations (Fig. 10g and h) demonstrated consistent LP characteristics, indicating the robustness of the optimized SCDs applied via spray for detecting sex differences and validating LPs as a reliable forensic tool.

The mechanism of nanocomposite (NC) film formation is illustrated in Fig. 11a , where hydrogen bonding occurs between the oxygen-containing functional groups of SCDs and the -OH groups in PVA. Notably, under 365 nm UV light, all SCDs@PVA NC films exhibited remarkable transparency in daylight, even at higher SCD concentrations, while emitting vivid colors characteristic of the encapsulated SCDs. As shown in Fig. 11b, FL (FL) intensity increased proportionally with SCD concentration. Additionally, the flexibility of these NC films was demonstrated through various folding scenarios, such as single and double folds, multiple folds, twisting, and rolling, as depicted in Fig. 11c. The bright red emission under UV light confirmed that PVA did not impair the photoluminescent (PL) efficiency of SCDs.

The optical properties of PVA and SCDs@PVA NC films were thoroughly analyzed using UV–Vis spectroscopy (Fig. 11d). The pristine PVA film displayed high transparency, transmitting 92.6% of visible light in the 400–800 nm range. As SCD concentrations in the PVA matrix increased, visible light transmittance gradually decreased [89]. Specifically, the optical transmittance at 550 nm dropped to 39.68%, 85.16%, 80.16%, and 81.96% for films containing increasing SCD concentrations (Table 3 ). This reduction in transparency results from the effective dispersion of SCDs within the PVA matrix, which minimally affects NC transmittance. Notably, characteristic absorption peaks for SCDs appeared in the SCDs@PVA films’ absorption spectra, confirming the successful doping of SCDs into the PVA matrix (indicated by green asterisks). The addition of SCDs also increased UV light absorption; even at low SCD concentrations (0.08 wt.%), the film demonstrated improved UV-blocking properties.

Further analysis of UV transmittance revealed that SCDs@PVA (0.08 wt.%) films exhibited excellent UV-shielding capabilities, absorbing 79.02% of UVB and 83.57% of UVA radiation, compared to pristine PVA, which only blocked 13.28% and 21% of UVB and UVA light, respectively. While adding more SCDs enhanced UV absorption, it slightly reduced transparency. These results suggest the potential of SCDs as effective UV absorbers for transparent films, providing UV protection without compromising visible light transparency [90].

The PL spectrum of SCDs@PVA NC films (0.02–0.08 wt.%) at 496 nm excitation exhibited a prominent peak centered at 610 nm, indicative of red FL, as shown in Fig. 11e. Chromaticity analysis (Fig. 11f) revealed shifts in CIE coordinates between PVA and SCDs@PVA films under different lighting conditions. While PVA coordinates shifted from (0.311, 0.304) in daylight to (0.253, 0.266) under UV, SCDs@PVA (0.10 wt.%) films showed a distinct shift from (0.629, 0.346) to (0.618, 0.245) [91]. Color measurements, facilitated by color mixing and calculator software, underscored the impact of SCDs on the film’s color properties.

To assess the mechanical stability of the flexible SCDs@PVA (0.08 wt.%) films, mechanical loads ranging from 0 to 5 tons were applied for 20 minutes each, as shown in Fig. 11g. Once the stress was removed, the films consistently maintained their properties under normal and UV light, indicating remarkable resilience. This durability positions them for applications in traffic signaling and solid-state lighting. Furthermore, PL experiments evaluated the PL intensity distribution in red-emitting films under varying mechanical strains (0–5 tons), illustrated in Fig. 11h. At 496 nm excitation, PL intensity within a designated circular area exhibited uniform distribution across all films, suggesting potential for flexible display applications where flexibility is critical for optimal performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV shielding behavior of the SCDs@PVA NCs films
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 12a provides a schematic illustrating the UV-blocking mechanism of SCDs within a polymer film. A simple experimental setup (Fig. 12b) was used to evaluate the UV shielding capacity of the SCDs NC film, with a UV digital lux meter positioned 3 cm below the NC film holder [92]. The light source was either a UV lamp or natural daylight. UV light intensity transmitted through the polymer film was recorded using the UV meter, and films with increasing SCD content were tested under both lighting conditions (Fig. 12c–f).

A pure PVA polymer film, without SCDs, blocked only ∼16.24% of UV light. In contrast, a PVA film with 0.02 wt.% SCDs showed approximately 39.59% UV-blocking under a UV lamp. Films with SCD concentrations of 0.04 wt.%, 0.06 wt.%, and 0.08 wt.% absorbed approximately 44.29%, 48.99%, and 59.73% of UV light, respectively (Fig. 12c). A similar test in daylight confirmed these findings (Fig. 12d), with the optimal concentration of 0.08 wt.% providing the highest UV shielding. This result aligns with the UV–vis absorption spectrum, where a distinct absorption peak within the UV range confirms the UV-blocking efficacy of the 0.08 wt.% SCDs@PVA film.

Thicker polymer films were prepared with 0.08 wt.% to enhance UV shielding further.% SCDs in polymer solutions of 8, 10, 12, and 14 mL volumes. These films were tested for UV shielding efficiency with increasing thickness under UV lamp and daylight conditions (Fig. 12e and f). Under daylight, direct UV light intensity was recorded at 3300 μW/cm². As seen in Fig. 12e, the 100 μm (8 mL) thick SCDs@PVA film absorbed about 75.75% of UV (800 μW/cm²), while the same thickness of pure PVA blocked only 22.72% (2550 μW/cm²) of UV. A thicker 250 μm (14 mL) PVA film provided a maximum UV shielding of 30.90% under daylight (Fig. S9a, Supporting Information), underscoring the significance of SCDs in enhancing UV shielding.

The trend showed that UV absorption increased with film thickness. For instance, a 150 μm (10 mL) SCDs@PVA film absorbed approximately 80.30% (650 μW/cm²) of UV light. The thickest SCDs@PVA polymer film (250 μm, 14 mL) achieved nearly 97.57% UV shielding (80 μW/cm²) in daylight (Fig. 12e). Additionally, the UV shielding effectiveness of varying film thicknesses (100, 150, 200, and 250 μm) was evaluated under a UV lamp, as illustrated in Fig. 12f. The UV lamp emitted an intensity of 745 μW/cm². As shown in Fig. S9b (Supporting Information), the pristine PVA film (250 μm) blocked about 17.44% (615 μW/cm²) of UV light, whereas SCDs@PVA films of 150 μm and 250 μm thickness shielded approximately 79.86% (150 μW/cm²) and 95.30% (35 μW/cm²) of UV light, respectively.

These findings demonstrate that UV shielding efficacy increases with film thickness, regardless of light source [93], highlighting the importance of film thickness for enhancing UV-blocking properties. Quantitative UV shielding measurements using a digital lux meter revealed that the substantial UV protection in SCDs@PVA films is due to effective light scattering and reflection by SCDs, which convert UV photons into heat. The pristine PVA film exhibited a UV shielding capacity of approximately 16.24%, while the SCDs@PVA dispersed film reached 95.30%. This substantial improvement in UV shielding, exceeding 79%, is attributed to SCD surface modification, which facilitates their dispersion within the polymer matrix. Consequently, 0.08 wt.% SCDs@PVA films show promise as effective UV-blocking materials, offering a viable alternative for protecting UV-sensitive objects.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical properties of SCDs@ PVA nanocomposite films
ce:section.ce:para: Tensile testing was conducted to assess the impact of SCDs (0.02–0.08 wt.%) on the mechanical properties of PVA films, with results presented in Fig. S10a and Table S3 in the Supporting Information. The pristine PVA film demonstrated a tensile strength of 70.0 MPa and an elongation at a break of 190.1% upon incorporating 0.02 wt.% SCDs, the tensile strength of the SCDs@PVA nanocomposite (NC) film slightly decreased, while the elongation at break increased significantly to 260.2%. Further additions of SCDs (0.04–0.08 wt.%) maintained relatively constant tensile strength yet consistently enhanced elongation at break. This increase in elongation may be attributed to the formation of stronger hydrogen bonding interactions between PVA and SCDs compared to those in pure PVA [94].

The WVP of PVA and SCDs@PVA NC films was also evaluated (Fig. S10b, Supporting Information). Pristine PVA exhibited a WVP of approximately 5.55×10−10 g-m/Pa-s-m². With increasing SCD content (0.02–0.06 wt.%), WVP gradually decreased to around 3.60 × 10−10, 3.56 × 10−10, and 3.50 × 10−10 g-m/Pa-s-m², respectively. This improvement in water vapor barrier performance is likely due to the dispersion of SCDs, which reduces the active area for water vapor transmission through the film [95]; however, at 0.08 wt.% SCDs, WVP increased to approximately 4.5 × 10−10 g-m/Pa-s-m², potentially due to enhanced hydrophilicity, where some water absorption affected the WVP measurements. Overall, the water vapor barrier properties of SCDs@PVA films are superior to those of many biopolymer films, such as chitosan, gelatin, and corn starch [96–98]. Improved water vapor barriers in packaging materials help reduce moisture loss, making these films particularly advantageous for preserving foods like fresh fruits and vegetables.

Fig. S11 in the Supporting Information illustrates the films' luminescent and mechanical flexibility characteristics the 0.08 wt.% SCDs@PVA film demonstrated sufficient flexibility, bending smoothly from 0 to 180° without cracking. Notably, even after 1500 bending cycles, the film PL intensity remained stable, underscoring the durability of SCDs@PVA NC films. This resilience largely stems from the inherent flexibility of PVA, making SCDs@PVA NC films well-suited for applications in flexible displays.

We investigated the shape memory and FL (FL) properties of SCDs@PVA NC films, focusing on hydrogen bond-based molecular switching. These films were prepared as thin strips and subjected to bending tests to observe their shape recovery response to heat, pH, and water stimuli [35]. Initially, the strips were deformed by heating in an 80°C oven for 5 minutes and then fixed at room temperature (RT). As shown in Fig. 13a , immersing the deformed SCDs@PVA strips in DI water at RT allowed them to gradually revert to their original shape over approximately 120 seconds, demonstrating an environmental stimulus-driven shape memory effect. In a neutral aqueous solution, the SCDs@PVA sample displayed a gradual decrease in FL, although not a complete loss. Fig. 13b and c depict the behavior of the deformed SCDs@PVA strips under acidic and alkaline conditions. Under alkaline conditions, the strips exhibited a shape recovery angle of 146° within 120 seconds, accompanied by increased FL intensity. Conversely, in acidic conditions, the strips recovered to a 150° angle in 90 seconds, with FL decreasing and eventually vanishing as the strips returned to their initial shape. Temperature also influenced shape recovery, as immersing the distorted strips in a heated environment (70°C) led to rapid shape recovery to a 153° angle in 10 seconds (Fig. 13d).

The FL response to pH changes is attributed to the ionization of carboxyl groups on the CDs surface, where protonation in acidic conditions reduces FL due to increased surface defects, while deprotonation in alkaline conditions enhances FL by reducing defects and increasing the conjugation of the SCDs system. This protonation and deprotonation process also affects hydrogen bonding between SCDs and PVA, resulting in lower shape recovery rates in alkaline environments compared to acidic ones. The ability of SCDs@PVA NC films to modify shape recovery rates and FL intensity in response to pH and temperature highlights their potential applications in specific conditions.

To further assess shape memory behavior, a quatrefoil-shaped SCDs@PVA sample was folded, allowing the petals to quickly bloom into a closed shape within 100 seconds due to water-induced recovery (Fig. 13e). This remarkable water-responsive shape memory property is complemented by the visible red FL emitted by SCDs under UV light (365 nm). Standard bending-recovery tests were conducted under various pH conditions to quantify the water-induced shape memory effect, as shown in Fig. 13f. All NC samples displayed outstanding water-induced shape memory, with recovery ratios exceeding 93%. However, response times were significantly affected by water pH. In acidic water (pH=3), complete recovery occurred within 60 seconds, while in neutral water (pH=7), recovery took 120 seconds, and in alkaline water (pH=11), the original shape was restored in 180 seconds. In contrast, a pure PVA film achieved only a 30% recovery ratio after 180 seconds in neutral pH water (Fig. 13g), underscoring the superior shape memory effect of SCDs@PVA NCs compared to pure PVA. These findings confirm the enhanced shape memory performance of SCDs@PVA NCs, highlighting their potential for applications where responsive, tunable shape memory and FL properties are desired.

Fresh strawberries were meticulously inspected for uniformity in size, color, shape, texture, and absence of visible defects or decay before coating. After washing and drying, the strawberries underwent a double coating with a solution of PVA and 0.08 wt.% SCDs@PVA using the dip-coating method [99]. Both coated and uncoated strawberries were then hung on designated holders and left to dry at room temperature (26°C) and refrigerated conditions (-4°C). For visual monitoring, photographs were taken at regular intervals over eight days. Additionally, mold growth on strawberries was visually assessed, using strawberries as a model for perishable items needing specific storage conditions. Fig. 14a shows a strawberry half-coated with 0.08 wt.% SCDs@PVA, monitored for mold development. These experiments were repeated with at least four strawberries to ensure reliability.

Strawberries coated with PVA and 0.08 wt.% SCDs@PVA—chosen for their enhanced antibacterial activity—were stored at RT and in the refrigerator, considering the higher biological activity associated with SCD-integrated PVA films. Photographs of coated and uncoated strawberries were taken using a smartphone to track visual changes, mold formation, and moisture retention. By the eighth day, both PVA-coated and uncoated strawberries showed visible mold contamination, while those coated with 0.08 wt.% SCDs@PVA, particularly those stored at RT, remained mold-free (Fig. 14b). Strawberries coated with 0.08 wt.% SCDs@PVA in refrigerated storage showed no mold by day eight, unlike the PVA-coated and uncoated samples. Moreover, SCDs@PVA-coated strawberries retained more moisture and a vibrant red color (Fig. 14c).

To further assess the coating's effectiveness compared to pure PVA, another set of strawberries was partially coated with 0.08 wt.% SCDs@PVA and monitored at RT (Fig. 14d). As anticipated, the coated area remained mold-free for seven days, while mold developed on the uncoated side by day five. These results highlight the effectiveness of the 0.08 wt.% SCDs@PVA coating prevents mold growth and extends surface freshness. The semi-coated strawberries retained moisture longer due to reduced water activity, even as the uncoated portion experienced drying. These findings suggest that incorporating SCDs into packaging films or coatings protects food by reducing mold growth, bacterial activity, spoilage, and moisture loss during storage. Using these films or coatings with SCDs in solution form offers an economical and environmentally friendly approach to extending the shelf life of perishable goods like strawberries. This strategy can help mitigate food spoilage-related financial losses and health risks, promoting sustainable food preservation solutions [100].

Due to their exceptional FL (FL) properties, stability, affordability, and eco-friendly synthesis, red-emitting SCDs have significant potential as fluorescent inks for AC applications [101]. Fig. 15a illustrates the construction of an AC label using inkjet printing and a simple brush application technique. SCDs (30 μM) are effective illumination materials for secure AC, providing practical and safe information encryption and decryption when exposed to UV light at 365 nm. This capability makes SCDs an efficient solution for protecting sensitive data [102].

To test feasibility, inkjet printing technology created luminous designs on craft paper, cellulose paper, and cardboard. These patterns were invisible under average daylight, ensuring effective information encryption without visible traces. However, bright red patterns emerged under UV 365 nm light, demonstrating the efficacy of SCDs (30 μM) as fluorescent ink for security applications (Fig. 15b). Furthermore, a 120-day storage test validated the stability of the AC patterns made with SCDs (30 μM) fluorescent ink (Fig. 15c). Even after extended storage, the emission color remained consistent, affirming the ink's resilience to environmental factors and suitability for secure applications.

Photostability is crucial for practical applications of SCDs (30 μM). Fig. 15d shows the remarkable photo-stability of AC patterns before and after 120 minutes of 365 nm UV exposure. The FL intensity varied minimally, indicating the high photo-stability of the SCDs (30 μM) solution. In addition, Fig. 15e demonstrates AC patterns drawn on painted wood and ceramic using SCDs (30 μM) as fluorescent ink, visible only under UV light. When illuminated at 365 nm, the ink produced vivid red-emitting AC patterns, highlighting its advanced FL AC potential. The stability of these SCDs-based patterns over 120 days, as shown in Fig. 15f, further confirmed their resistance to environmental degradation, making them ideal for secure AC applications. Overall, the robust emission and remarkable photo-stability of SCDs (30 μM) FL inks make them a promising choice for secure and durable AC applications, withstanding prolonged UV exposure and environmental conditions, as illustrated in Fig. 15g.

The biocompatibility of SCDs is a critical factor for their potential medical applications. To evaluate the cytotoxicity of SCDs, SH-SY-5Y cells were cultured at a density of 5×10³ cells per well in Ham’s F12K medium under 5% CO₂ and 95% humidity. Various SCDs concentrations (0, 50, 75, 100, 125, and 150 μg/mL) were applied to the cells over 48 hours. Cell viability was then assessed using the MTT assay, with results indicating minimal toxicity, as shown in Fig. 16 . SH-SY-5Y cell viability remained above 70% even at the highest 150 μg/mL concentration, suggesting excellent biocompatibility. These findings confirm that the synthesized SCDs exhibit low cytotoxicity, likely due to their natural source and the biocompatible, degradable properties imparted by the synthesis method [103,104]. This compatibility underscores the suitability of SCDs for biomedical applications, expanding their potential for safe use in medical contexts.

This study presents the synthesis and multifunctional applications of red-emissive carbon dots (SCDs) derived from Spinach leaves via a green solvothermal route. Unlike most plant-derived carbon dots that exhibit fluorescence near the chlorophyll emission range (660–680 nm), the SCDs synthesized here demonstrate a unique excitation-independent emission centered at 610 nm. This deviation is attributed to a deliberate multi-step extraction and thermal degradation process that removes native chlorophyll pigments and facilitates the formation of novel conjugated aromatic domains and surface functional groups.

The observed photoluminescence behavior arises from n–π* and π–π* transitions, as confirmed by UV–Vis absorption peaks at 335 nm and 496 nm. Combined spectroscopic analyses, including FTIR, XPS, Raman, and fluorescence lifetime studies, reveal that the red emission originates from newly engineered surface states and functional groups (e.g., C=O, C=N, NH₂), rather than from residual chlorophyll-based porphyrins. This structural transformation yields SCDs with high quantum yield, robust fluorescence stability, and photobleaching resistance over extended durations, ensuring their suitability for demanding applications.

The versatility of SCDs is deeply rooted in their intrinsic optical properties, allowing deployment across diverse domains. At the core of their multifunctionality is the strong red PL that enhances their adaptability in applications such as advanced encryption, security printing, fluorescent films, UV protection, and forensic science. The stable and unique FL of SCDs enables robust security measures in AC and encryption applications, as their distinct emission spectra create an encryption layer that is challenging to replicate.

When integrated into polymer matrices, SCDs produce highly visible fluorescent films under UV light, with UV-blocking capabilities that benefit security tags and protective coatings. Their UV-blocking properties also extend to food packaging, enhancing shelf life by preventing UV-induced degradation of packaged foods. The biocompatibility and non-toxic nature of SCDs make them safe for food-related uses. In forensic science, the strong PL properties of SCDs improve the visibility of LFPs and LLPs under UV light, underscoring their value in fingerprint detection and cheiloscopy. The adaptability of SCDs to security and forensic applications reflects the interconnected nature of their underlying mechanisms. The shared PL mechanisms make SCDs suitable for applications across various fields, from protecting food from UV light to enhancing security labels and detecting biometric patterns.

The red-emissive PL of SCDs originates from surface passivation, quantum confinement effects, and specific functional groups on their surface. These factors together yield a high quantum yield, as shown by spectroscopic data and theoretical calculations, with these functional groups playing a crucial role in structuring the energy bands to facilitate efficient emission pathways. In fingerprint detection, SCDs interact effectively with latent fingerprint (LFP) components like amino acids and lipids, enabling enhanced FL upon contact. This study explores the performance of SCDs under various environmental conditions, focusing on the physical and chemical interactions that contribute to their stability and sensitivity, making them suitable for LFP visualization.

The distinct emission spectra and high photostability of SCDs support their use in AC applications. Their specific PL response at different excitation wavelengths adds a sophisticated layer for information encryption, enhancing security and reliability. Due to their UV absorption and scattering properties, SCDs exhibit strong UV-blocking capabilities when incorporated into flexible films. The interaction between SCDs and the polymer matrix enhances UV-blocking efficiency, as confirmed by UV-Vis absorption spectra demonstrating the film’s stability and protective qualities.

This study demonstrates the synthesis of red-emissive SCDs, revealing their broad applicability as sustainable, multifunctional nanomaterials in fields such as forensic science, food preservation, anti-counterfeiting, and UV protection. By leveraging the unique optical and physicochemical properties of these SCDs, we achieved significant advancements in latent fingerprint visualization, secure information encryption, and effective UV shielding, showcasing the material's versatility. The SCDs integrated into PVA films form stable, flexible nanocomposites, which provide robust UV protection and support extended shelf life for perishable foods. Additionally, cytotoxicity tests indicate that these SCDs are biocompatible, enhancing their suitability for consumer and biomedical applications. The coffee-ring effect and electrostatic interactions inherent to SCDs contribute to their stable FL in forensic applications, while their resilience to photobleaching affirms their potential for long-term use in security and packaging solutions. This work highlights Spinach derived CDs as promising candidates for environmentally friendly, high-performance materials across diverse applications. Future research may explore the expansion of SCD functionalities in emerging fields, potentially including advanced biomedicine and next-generation photonic devices.
